topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating monotony, mirroring the churning in my stomach. I stared at the application form, the ink seeming to mock my efforts. Liam, my supposed best friend since kindergarten, had applied for the Senior Analyst position, the very role I’d been angling for. A sharp, unpleasant tightening began in my chest, radiating outward. I reread the job description, my jaw clenching so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. It felt like a deliberate slight, a calculated move by him to sideline me. When I finally submitted my own materials, my hand trembled slightly, a betrayal of the composure I desperately tried to maintain. I could practically taste the bitterness rising in my throat.

[story 2]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. My gaze kept flicking to the screen where Liam’s application summary was highlighted, a smug little beacon of success. Every word he’d written felt like an accusation. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and began to write, but my handwriting was a frantic mess, jagged lines betraying the internal turmoil. There was a distinct, cold pressure building behind my eyes. I wanted to slam the laptop shut, to walk out and let the frustration spill over, but I forced myself to keep typing. The silence of the room amplified the sound of my own anxious breathing.

[story 3]

"He really thought he could pull that off," I muttered under my breath, the words barely audible above the keyboard clicking. The air in the small apartment felt thick, suffocating. I paced the worn rug, my movements jerky and sharp. Every time I looked at the framed photo of us from college, a wave of profound resentment washed over me. It wasn't just about the job; it was about the perceived betrayal, the sense that my efforts were being dismissed in favor of someone else. I stopped pacing abruptly, planting my feet firmly on the floor, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to steady the tremor that was starting in my limbs.
